items.text: Which lifestyle or pharmacological interventions produce a measurable slowing of biological aging as captured by epigenetic clocks such as DunedinPACE?
Do NAD+ precursors and senolytic compounds extend human healthspan, or do their benefits stop at biomarkers without changing functional outcomes?
What evidence-based combination of diet, sleep, and exercise minimizes all-cause mortality and the risk of age-related disease?
Biological aging can be quantified through biomarkers (epigenetic clocks, organ-specific aging scores) and meaningfully slowed through disciplined, measured intervention.
Sleep quality and metabolic markers — continuous glucose, ApoB, and inflammatory markers — are foundational, high-leverage levers for healthspan.

In self-tracked data, therapeutic plasma exchange was discontinued after analysis showed no measurable benefit to biological-age markers — interventions are kept only if they move a measured endpoint.
A structured diet, exercise, sleep, and supplement protocol was associated with a measured slowing of aging pace (DunedinPACE ~0.69) and improvement across a majority of tracked biomarkers.

Run two comprehensive biomarker panels per year across 100+ biomarkers and 70+ organ systems, trialing tier-3 experimental therapies and retaining only those that improve measured biological-age endpoints.
